diagrams.title: The Swiss Roll Manifold Example
diagrams.description: A 3D 'Swiss Roll' dataset demonstrating how data points lying on a curved 2D surface can be embedded in higher-dimensional space. The diagram shows the rolled-up manifold in 3D and its unrolled 2D representation, illustrating the manifold assumption in dimensionality reduction.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the manifold hypothesis: high-dimensional data often lies on or near a lower-dimensional manifold, which is the foundational concept enabling dimensionality reduction.

diagrams.title: Dimensionality Reduction Methods Comparison
diagrams.description: A comparison of how different dimensionality reduction techniques transform the same high-dimensional data. Shows PCA (linear projection), t-SNE (non-linear local structure preservation), and UMAP (global structure preservation with topology), demonstrating the trade-offs between computational efficiency, preservation of local vs global structure, and interpretability.
elements: UMAP preserve both local and global structure
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the different approaches to dimensionality reduction and when each method is most appropriate, helping students understand that no single method is optimal for all scenarios.

diagrams.title: Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Dimensionality
diagrams.description: A visual demonstration showing the difference between the intrinsic (true) dimensionality of data and the extrinsic (observed) dimensionality. Using a rolled paper or S-curve example, the diagram contrasts the ambient space dimension with the manifold dimension, showing how many 'effective' degrees of freedom the data actually has.
elements: Data on a 1D curved path within 3D
diagrams.purpose: Clarifies the fundamental concept that the number of variables in a dataset does not equal its intrinsic complexity, a key insight for understanding why dimensionality reduction is possible and meaningful.
